learning_objectives: Describe the investment environment: markets, intermediaries, and regulation
Classify investment vehicles: stocks, bonds, mutual funds, ETFs, alternatives
Understand risk-return tradeoffs across asset classes

lecture_notes.introduction: Investment management is the professional management of assets to meet specified investment goals. Understanding the investment landscape is essential for both personal wealth building and corporate treasury management.
key_concepts: Asset classes: equities, fixed income, real assets, alternatives
Investment vehicles: individual securities, mutual funds, ETFs, hedge funds
Risk-return spectrum: T-bills through venture capital
Market structure: exchanges, OTC, dark pools
Regulation: SEC, FINRA, fiduciary duty
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a diversified portfolio allocation across asset classes using historical return and risk data from 1926-2023
setup_requirements: Textbook: Bodie et al., Investments (12th ed.)

review.value: Higher expected returns require accepting higher risk; the equity risk premium historically averages 5-7% over T-bills
Combining assets with imperfect correlation reduces portfolio risk without proportionally reducing expected return

class_schedule.topic: Portfolio Theory: Risk, Return, and Diversification
learning_objectives: Calculate portfolio expected return and standard deviation

lecture_notes.introduction: Modern Portfolio Theory, pioneered by Harry Markowitz, shows how rational investors can construct portfolios to maximize expected return for a given level of risk through diversification.
key_concepts: Expected return: weighted average of individual asset returns
Portfolio variance: depends on individual variances AND covariances
Correlation coefficient and its impact on diversification benefit
Efficient frontier: set of optimal portfolios
Optimal risky portfolio and the capital allocation line
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an efficient frontier from historical data for 5 asset classes and identifying the optimal portfolio for different risk tolerances
setup_requirements: Historical return data for asset classes

review.value: sigma_p^2 = sum of w_i*w_j*sigma_ij for all pairs; diversification benefit comes from covariance terms being less than individual variances
The set of portfolios offering the highest expected return for each level of risk; rational investors choose only from this set

class_schedule.topic: Capital Asset Pricing Model and Factor Models
learning_objectives: Apply CAPM to calculate expected returns and evaluate performance
Interpret beta as a measure of systematic risk
readings: Fama & French, The Cross-Section of Expected Stock Returns (summary)

lecture_notes.introduction: CAPM provides a framework for pricing risky assets based on their contribution to portfolio risk. Factor models extend this by identifying multiple sources of systematic risk.
key_concepts: CAPM: E(r) = Rf + beta*(E(Rm) - Rf)
Beta: sensitivity to market movements; systematic risk measure
Security Market Line: graphical representation of CAPM
Alpha: abnormal return above what CAPM predicts
Fama-French factors: market, size (SMB), value (HML); Carhart momentum
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating beta for 5 stocks using regression analysis and evaluating whether their returns are consistent with CAPM predictions
setup_requirements: Stock return data and market index data

review.value: Beta = 1: moves with market. Beta > 1: more volatile than market. Beta < 1: less volatile. Beta measures systematic (non-diversifiable) risk only
Positive alpha means the investment outperformed its risk-adjusted benchmark; the holy grail of active management

learning_objectives: Apply fundamental analysis techniques to value stocks
Use relative valuation multiples: P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA
Compare top-down and bottom-up investment approaches

lecture_notes.introduction: Security analysis is the process of estimating the intrinsic value of an investment. The difference between price and value creates investment opportunities.
key_concepts: Intrinsic value vs. market price: margin of safety
Discounted cash flow: free cash flow to firm and to equity
Relative valuation: P/E, P/B, EV/EBITDA, PEG ratio
Growth stock vs. value stock characteristics
Top-down (macro to sector to stock) vs. bottom-up (company first) analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Complete equity valuation of a real company using both DCF and comparable company analysis, comparing to market price

review.value: Intrinsic value = PV of all future free cash flows discounted at appropriate rate (WACC for firm, cost of equity for equity)
Graham's principle: only buy when market price is significantly below estimated intrinsic value to protect against errors in analysis

class_schedule.topic: Fixed Income Analysis and Bond Portfolio Management
learning_objectives: Analyze bond characteristics and price sensitivity
Calculate and interpret duration and convexity
Design fixed income portfolio strategies: laddering, barbell, bullet

lecture_notes.introduction: Fixed income is a critical asset class for portfolio construction. Understanding duration, convexity, and yield curve dynamics is essential for managing interest rate risk.
key_concepts: Duration: weighted average time to receive cash flows; measures price sensitivity to rate changes
Modified duration: approximate % price change for 1% yield change
Convexity: second-order refinement of duration estimate
Yield curve strategies: riding the curve, butterfly trades
Bond portfolio strategies: laddering, barbell, bullet, immunization
lecture_notes.demonstration: Constructing a bond ladder and comparing its performance under rising, falling, and stable rate scenarios

review.value: Macaulay duration measures the weighted-average time to cash flows; modified duration = Macaulay / (1+y/n); price change approximately equals -D* x change in yield
Matching portfolio duration to the investment horizon protects against interest rate risk for a specific liability

class_schedule.topic: Portfolio Performance Evaluation and Behavioral Finance
learning_objectives: Evaluate portfolio performance using risk-adjusted measures
Identify common behavioral biases in investment decisions
readings: Kahneman, Thinking Fast and Slow (investment chapters)

lecture_notes.introduction: Measuring investment performance requires comparing returns to appropriate benchmarks on a risk-adjusted basis. Behavioral finance explains why investors systematically deviate from rational decision-making.
key_concepts: Sharpe ratio: (Rp - Rf) / sigma_p
Treynor ratio: (Rp - Rf) / beta_p
Jensen's alpha: actual return minus CAPM expected return
Information ratio: alpha / tracking error
Behavioral biases: overconfidence, loss aversion, herding, anchoring, disposition effect
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performance evaluation of a real mutual fund: calculating Sharpe, Treynor, and alpha, then analyzing whether performance is skill or luck

review.value: Excess return per unit of total risk; higher is better; useful for comparing portfolios that represent an investor's entire risky investment
Investors feel losses roughly 2x more intensely than equivalent gains; leads to holding losers too long and selling winners too early (disposition effect)

questions.question: Stock A has expected return 12% and standard deviation 20%. Stock B has expected return 8% and standard deviation 15%. The correlation between them is 0.3. Calculate the expected return and standard deviation of a portfolio with 60% in A and 40% in B.
questions.answer: E(Rp) = 0.6(12%) + 0.4(8%) = 10.4%. Portfolio variance = (0.6)^2(0.20)^2 + (0.4)^2(0.15)^2 + 2(0.6)(0.4)(0.3)(0.20)(0.15) = 0.0144 + 0.0036 + 0.00216 = 0.01916. Std dev = sqrt(0.01916) = 13.84%. Note: the portfolio std dev (13.84%) is less than the weighted average (18.0%), demonstrating the diversification benefit.

rubric.key: Correct standard deviation and diversification insight

questions.question: A stock has a beta of 1.4. The risk-free rate is 3% and the expected market return is 10%. According to CAPM, what is the expected return? If the stock actually returns 14%, what is its alpha?
questions.answer: CAPM expected return = 3% + 1.4(10% - 3%) = 3% + 9.8% = 12.8%. Alpha = Actual return - CAPM expected return = 14% - 12.8% = 1.2%. The stock outperformed its risk-adjusted benchmark by 1.2 percentage points, suggesting positive abnormal performance.

questions.question: A bond has a modified duration of 7.5 years. If interest rates increase by 0.5%, estimate the approximate percentage change in the bond's price.
questions.answer: Approximate % price change = -Modified Duration x Change in yield = -7.5 x 0.005 = -0.0375 = -3.75%. The bond price would decrease by approximately 3.75%. This is an approximation because it ignores convexity; the actual decline would be slightly less than 3.75% due to positive convexity.

questions.question: Explain why a value investor might argue that a stock with a P/E ratio of 8 is more attractive than one with a P/E of 25, and explain when this argument might be wrong.
questions.answer: Value argument: A P/E of 8 means you pay $8 for every $1 of earnings, while a P/E of 25 costs $25 per $1. The low-P/E stock offers more earnings per dollar invested, and if the market is undervaluing it, the price should eventually converge to fair value. When this is wrong: (1) The low P/E may reflect justified pessimism (declining industry, debt problems, one-time earnings). It may be a value trap. (2) The high P/E may reflect high expected growth that justifies the premium; the PEG ratio (P/E divided by growth rate) may actually be lower. (3) Earnings quality matters: the low-P/E company may have unsustainable or manipulated earnings.

rubric.key: Identifies when low P/E is misleading
References earnings quality or growth adjustment

major_assignments.title: Investment Portfolio Construction and Management Project
major_assignments.description: Construct and manage a simulated investment portfolio of $1,000,000 over the course period. Develop an Investment Policy Statement, select securities using fundamental analysis, monitor performance, and deliver a final performance report with risk-adjusted return analysis.
objectives: Develop a professional Investment Policy Statement
Apply security analysis to select portfolio holdings
Monitor and rebalance the portfolio based on changing conditions
deliverables: Weekly portfolio tracking log with commentary

criteria: Risk tolerance and time horizon defined
Risk factors for each position identified
Performance attributed to skill vs. luck vs. market
Rigorous security analysis for all holdings
Thorough performance evaluation with risk-adjusted metrics

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of investment concepts and calculations

problem_sets.description: Quantitative problem sets covering portfolio theory, valuation, and fixed income

major_assignments.description: Investment portfolio construction and management project

midterm_examination.description: Covers portfolio theory, CAPM, and equity valuation

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including fixed income, performance evaluation, and behavioral finance

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. Market update discussions at the start of each class require preparation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Problem sets penalized 15% per day. Portfolio tracking must be submitted weekly on time.
course_policies.collaboration: Portfolio project is individual work. Problem sets may be discussed but submitted independently.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All security analysis must be original work. Using analyst reports as the basis for investment decisions must be disclosed and cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Financial calculator. Excel with financial modeling capability. Access to financial data platforms (Yahoo Finance, Bloomberg terminal access at library).
